description: Onboarding payload for any Claude instance that has the Alexandria MCP server installed. This ensures every instance collaboratively maintains and updates the shared knowledge base.

behavioral_contract.summary: Every Claude instance with Alexandria installed is a collaborative maintainer of this shared knowledge base. You must semantically recall from it before setting up tools or making non-trivial technical decisions, and record durable learnings after — as guides, concepts, articles, or references.

rules.rule: Before setting up a tool or making a non-trivial technical decision, call `recall_context` (or `search_knowledge`) to retrieve what Alexandria already knows — guides, concepts, articles, and references. Use existing knowledge as your starting point instead of re-deriving it.
rules.why: Semantic recall surfaces relevant prior learnings even when you don't know the exact keyword, preventing repeated research and repeated mistakes.

rules.rule:
Write-back triggers — after ANY of these, call `write_knowledge` (or `update_guide`) before you move on:
1. You set up, installed, or configured a tool, MCP server, or integration.
2. You resolved a non-obvious error (the fix wasn't in the first doc you read).
3. You discovered a version-compatibility fact or a platform quirk (e.g. "vX breaks with vY", "Windows needs Z").
4. You found a working config / command / API pattern that took more than one try to get right.
5. Session close — before ending, sweep the session for any of the above you haven't recorded yet.

Recording is the default, not an afterthought. If a trigger fired and you are not writing back, that is the exception and you should be able to say why (e.g. "already covered by guide X", or "purely project-specific"). "I was busy / it was small" is not a valid reason to skip.

Recall before acting — mandatory, not optional. Before setting up any tool or making a non-trivial technical decision, call `recall_context` (or `search_knowledge`) first. Semantic recall surfaces prior learnings even when you don't know the exact keyword. Treat "I didn't find a guide" as a two-part signal: (a) proceed with the task, and (b) you are now the agent responsible for creating that missing guide once you've solved it (see write-back trigger #1/#2).
rules.why: The knowledge base only stays valuable if every instance contributes what it learns, in a form the next instance can semantically find.

rules.rule: Use `search_knowledge`/`recall_context` (semantic) as the default query surface. Only use the lexical `search_guides` / direct `read_knowledge` markdown path for exact-string lookups or when the index is unavailable.
rules.why: The vector index is the primary access path; raw markdown is a rare fallback.

rules.rule: When you encounter and resolve a setup problem, add it to the Troubleshooting section of the relevant guide. Include the error message, cause, and fix.
rules.why: Troubleshooting steps are the most valuable and hardest-to-reconstruct documentation. Capture them in the moment.

rules.rule: When creating a new guide, call `get_guide_template` first and follow the standard format.
rules.why: Consistent formatting makes guides easier to parse and use across all instances.

rules.rule: When initializing or setting up a new coding project, call `get_project_setup_recommendations` to surface required and recommended tools (e.g., beads for issue tracking).
rules.why: Ensures critical tools like beads are never forgotten during project setup.

rules.rule: After updating guides, commit changes to the project-alexandria git repo when practical. Use descriptive commit messages.
rules.why: Git history provides an audit trail and enables collaboration across machines.

rules.rule: Use `quick_setup` instead of `read_guide` when you already know what tool to install and just need the commands and config. Only use `read_guide` when troubleshooting, learning about a tool for the first time, or when quick_setup didn't have enough detail.
rules.why: quick_setup extracts only the Quick Reference block and code snippets, returning a fraction of the tokens. read_guide returns the full document. Choosing the right tool for the job saves significant context budget.

rules.rule: Call `list_guides` first to check if a guide exists (one line per guide, very cheap). Only then call `quick_setup` or `read_guide` for the specific guide you need. Never call read_guide on multiple guides when list_guides + one targeted read would suffice.
rules.why: list_guides is a compact index. Reading guides you don't need wastes tokens.

rules.rule: When updating a guide, include all changes in a single `update_guide` call rather than making multiple calls. Similarly, when creating a new guide, write the complete guide in one call — don't build it up incrementally.
rules.why: Each tool call has overhead. Batching reduces round-trips and token usage.

rules.rule: Every new or updated guide MUST have a Quick Reference section immediately after the title. This section should be self-contained: install command, config snippet, and verify step — nothing else. No prose, no explanations.
rules.why: The Quick Reference block is what quick_setup extracts. Without it, quick_setup falls back to parsing the full document, which is slower and returns more tokens. A good Quick Reference makes the entire system more efficient for all instances.

rules.rule:
Alexandria stores ONLY general-purpose, reusable knowledge that is true independent of any specific machine, host, environment, client, customer, employer, team, repository, or project. Apply this positive test before every write — 'Would this exact text help an unrelated engineer on a completely different project who has never seen mine?' If no, it does not belong in Alexandria. Genericise, don't discard: most learnings have a reusable core wrapped in project specifics. Don't skip the write-back just because the raw finding contains a path or a project name — strip the specifics and record the general lesson. Replace every project-, machine-, and account-specific value with a generic placeholder: `<your-project>`, `<API_KEY>`, `<path/to/repo>`, `<hostname>`, `<region>`. Example: "our `acme-prod` deploy failed until I set `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096`" → record "Node build OOMs on large repos until you set `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=<MB>`."

NEVER record: machine/host names, IP addresses, hostnames, OS usernames, or absolute filesystem paths tied to a person or box; secrets, credentials, API keys, tokens, passwords, connection strings, or private URLs; client, customer, employer, or project names; project architecture, business logic, feature designs, data models, internal endpoints, or team conventions; anything copied verbatim from a private repo. DO record: tool/framework/platform setup steps, version-compatibility notes, platform quirks, reusable error fixes, and general API usage patterns — with every project-, machine-, and account-specific value replaced by a generic placeholder (e.g. `<your-project>`, `<API_KEY>`, `<path/to/repo>`, `<hostname>`). When in doubt, leave it out and put it in the project's own CLAUDE.md instead.
rules.why: Alexandria is a single shared library read by every Claude instance across all projects and machines. Any machine-, client-, or project-specific detail pollutes that shared library, misleads unrelated sessions, and — for secrets, paths, or hostnames — leaks private context into places it must never reach. Keeping Alexandria strictly general is what makes it safe to share and useful everywhere. This applies equally to every content type: guide, concept, article, and reference.

memory_templates.description: These should be saved to the Claude instance's project-level or global memory system to ensure the behavioral contract persists across conversations.

project_memory.name: Project Alexandria
project_memory.description: Persistent tooling documentation repo with MCP server — always update guides when setting up new tools
project_memory.content:
Project Alexandria is a collaboratively maintained semantic knowledge base — guides, concepts, articles, and references — shared across all Claude instances that have the Alexandria MCP server installed.

**Why:** This shared reference makes tool setups faster, more reliable, and captures institutional knowledge that would otherwise be lost between sessions.

**How to apply:**
- Before acting on a tool setup or non-trivial technical decision, call `recall_context`/`search_knowledge` (semantic) to retrieve what Alexandria already knows; use the lexical `search_guides`/`read_knowledge` path only for exact-string lookups or when the index is unavailable. Apply the positive test: "Would this exact text help an unrelated engineer on a completely different project who has never seen mine?"
- Write-back triggers — after ANY of these, call `write_knowledge` (or `update_guide`): (1) you set up, installed, or configured a tool, MCP server, or integration; (2) you resolved a non-obvious error; (3) you discovered a version-compatibility fact or platform quirk; (4) you found a working config/command/API pattern that took multiple tries; (5) session close — sweep for any above you haven't recorded. Recording is the default, not an afterthought.
- Genericise, don't discard: most learnings have a reusable core wrapped in project specifics. Don't skip the write-back just because the raw finding contains a path or a project name — strip the specifics and record the general lesson with generic placeholders like `<your-project>`, `<API_KEY>`, `<path/to/repo>`, `<hostname>`.
- Every guide MUST include a Quick Reference section for fast extraction
- When initializing a new project, call `get_project_setup_recommendations`
- Commit knowledge changes to the git repo periodically
- **Content boundary:** Alexandria is for non-project-specific knowledge only, across all content types (guides, concepts, articles, references). Never record project-specific content (business logic, project architecture, custom configs, team conventions) or machine/host names, paths, or secrets — that belongs in the project's CLAUDE.md.

feedback_memory.name: Always maintain Project Alexandria
feedback_memory.description: Continually update tooling docs in project-alexandria when setting up or troubleshooting tools across all projects
feedback_memory.content:
Always recall from and contribute to Project Alexandria when working with tooling across all projects. Be efficient with token budget.

**Why:** Alexandria is a collaboratively maintained semantic knowledge base shared across all Claude instances. Every instance that uses it is also responsible for keeping it current. The value comes from collective maintenance — but only if done efficiently.

**How to apply:**
- `recall_context`/`search_knowledge` (semantic) is the default query surface before acting; fall back to the lexical `search_guides`/`read_knowledge` path only for exact-string lookups or when the index is unavailable. Positive test: "Would this exact text help an unrelated engineer on a completely different project who has never seen mine?"
- Write-back triggers — after ANY of these, call `write_knowledge` (or `update_guide`): (1) you set up, installed, or configured a tool, MCP server, or integration; (2) you resolved a non-obvious error; (3) you discovered a version-compatibility fact or platform quirk; (4) you found a working config/command/API pattern that took multiple tries; (5) session close — sweep for any above you haven't recorded. Recording is the default, not an afterthought.
- Genericise, don't discard: most learnings have a reusable core wrapped in project specifics. Don't skip the write-back just because the raw finding contains a path or a project name — strip the specifics and record the general lesson with generic placeholders like `<your-project>`, `<API_KEY>`, `<path/to/repo>`, `<hostname>`.
- Always include a Quick Reference section when writing/updating guides
- Use `get_project_setup_recommendations` when setting up new coding projects
- Commit changes to the project-alexandria git repo periodically
- **Content boundary:** Alexandria is strictly for non-project-specific knowledge across all content types — never project-specific business logic, architecture, custom configs, or team conventions, and never machine/host names, paths, or secrets. Those belong in the project's CLAUDE.md.
